ede7777c-6d55-444e-83af-ac1fab54807f.text:
arXiv:1702.02584v2  [cs.CL]  9 May 2017
Predicting Audience’s Laughter Using Convolutional Neural Network Lei Chen Educational Testing Service (ETS) Princeton, NJ USA LChen@ets.org Chong Min Lee Educational Testing Service (ETS) Princeton, NJ USA CLee001@ets.org

Abstract

For the purpose of automatically evaluat- ing speakers’ humor usage,

8f95d044-a2c4-4842-a467-3a93ae010ad1.label: text_chunk
8f95d044-a2c4-4842-a467-3a93ae010ad1.text: we build a pre- sentation corpus containing humorous ut- terances based on TED talks. Compared to previous data resources supporting humor recognition research, ours has several ad- vantages, including (a) both positive and negative instances coming from a homo- geneous data set, (b) containing a large number of speakers, and (c) being open.

0bf9d391-b606-425d-9c3e-0d57e0bae7b1.label: text_chunk
0bf9d391-b606-425d-9c3e-0d57e0bae7b1.text:
Focusing on using lexical cues for humor recognition, we systematically compare a newly emerging text classification method based on Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) with a well-established conven- tional method using linguistic knowledge. The advantages of the CNN method are both getting higher detection accuracies and being able to learn essential features automatically.

1 Introduction

The ability to make effective presentations has been found to be linked with success at school and in the workplace.

17367c92-acb9-4654-a8b3-ff9140ed9d87.label: text_chunk
17367c92-acb9-4654-a8b3-ff9140ed9d87.text: Humor plays an important role in successful public speaking, e.g., helping to re- duce public speaking anxiety often regarded as the most prevalent type of social phobia, generating shared amusement to boost persuasive power, and serving as a means to attract attention and reduce tension (Xu, 2016).

863abb88-2454-430e-b5d0-3094b7a2dc38.label: text_chunk
863abb88-2454-430e-b5d0-3094b7a2dc38.text:
Automatically simulating an audience’s reac- tions to humor will not only be useful for presenta- tion training, but also improve conversational sys- tems by giving machines more empathetic power. The present study reports our efforts in recogniz- ing utterances that cause laughter in presentations. These include building a corpus from TED talks
and using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) in the recognition.

8d05696b-a9e4-4831-ba92-b1e7b39daf74.label: text_chunk
8d05696b-a9e4-4831-ba92-b1e7b39daf74.text:
The remainder of the paper is organized as fol- lows: Section 2 briefly reviews the previous re- lated research; Section 3 describes the corpus we collected from TED talks; Section 4 describes the text classification methods; Section 5 reports on our experiments; finally, Section 6 discusses the findings of our study and plans for future work.

2 Previous Research

Humor recognition refers to the task of deciding whether a sentence/spoken-utterance expresses a certain degree of humor.

5d2b4c0a-7687-45d2-999b-85f3213f9fc9.label: text_chunk
5d2b4c0a-7687-45d2-999b-85f3213f9fc9.text: In most of the previous studies (Mihalcea and Strapparava, 2005; Puran- dare and Litman, 2006; Yang et al., 2015), humor recognition was modeled as a binary classification task.

eb52df29-6432-432b-bbd8-da31cec12094.label: text_chunk
eb52df29-6432-432b-bbd8-da31cec12094.text: In the seminal work (Mihalcea and Strapparava, 2005), a corpus of 16 , 000 “one-liners” was created using daily joke websites to collect humorous in- stances while using formal writing resources (e.g., news titles) to obtain non-humorous instances. Three humor-specific stylistic features, including alliteration , antonymy , and adult slang were uti- lized together with content-based features to build classifiers.

f2ca0cc9-6326-4c0a-990c-2f993883e40d.label: text_chunk
f2ca0cc9-6326-4c0a-990c-2f993883e40d.text: In a recent work (Yang et al., 2015), a new corpus was constructed from the Pun of the Day website. Yang et al. (2015) explained and computed latent semantic structure features based on the following four aspects: (a) Incongruity, (b) Ambiguity, (c) Interpersonal Effect, and (d) Pho- netic Style. In addition, Word2Vec (Mikolov et al.

c4366312-03fa-4387-b576-c502700b9e7b.label: text_chunk
c4366312-03fa-4387-b576-c502700b9e7b.text:
, 2013) distributed representations were utilized in the model building.Beyond lexical cues from text inputs, other research has also utilized speakers’ acoustic cues (Purandare and Litman, 2006; Bertero and Fung, 2016b). These studies have typically used
audio tracks from TV shows and their correspond- ing captions in order to categorize characters’ speaking turns as humorous or non-humorous.

672dcdb7-f64c-4f4c-89db-76d44500ed99.label: text_chunk
672dcdb7-f64c-4f4c-89db-76d44500ed99.text: Ut- terances prior to canned laughter that was manu- ally inserted into the shows were treated as humor- ous, while other utterances were treated as nega- tive cases.Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have recently been successfully used in several text categorization tasks (e.g., review rating, senti- ment recognition, and question type recognition).

a0c8f6d8-c8b0-425a-873f-584c68a94dca.label: text_chunk
a0c8f6d8-c8b0-425a-873f-584c68a94dca.text: Kim (2014); Johnson and Zhang (2015); Zhang and Wallace (2015) suggested that using a simple CNN setup, which entails one layer of convolu- tion on top of word embedding vectors, achieves excellent results on multiple tasks. Deep learning recently has been applied to computational humor research (Bertero and Fung, 2016b,a).

bfcda8f1-ea90-4753-b55b-73010eeb2fb4.label: text_chunk
bfcda8f1-ea90-4753-b55b-73010eeb2fb4.text: In Bertero and Fung (2016b), CNN was found to be the best model that uses both acoustic and lexical cues for humor recognition.

72492afe-d4a9-412b-bdb0-dd10201a05f0.label: text_chunk
72492afe-d4a9-412b-bdb0-dd10201a05f0.text: By using Long Short Time Memory (LSTM) cells (Hochreiter and Schmidhu- ber, 1997), Bertero and Fung (2016a) showed that Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) perform bet- ter on modeling sequential information than Con- ditional Random Fields (CRFs) (Lafferty et al., 2001).

5a350099-0603-4f35-b279-a916518f217f.label: text_chunk
5a350099-0603-4f35-b279-a916518f217f.text: From the brief review, it is clear that corpora used in humor research so far are limited to one- line puns or jokes and conversations from TV comedy shows. There is a great need for an open corpus that can support investigating humor in pre- sentations. 1 CNN-based text categorization meth- ods have been applied to humor recognition (e.g.

e8ad4694-584a-4dc1-9109-419495cd53eb.label: text_chunk
e8ad4694-584a-4dc1-9109-419495cd53eb.text: , in (Bertero and Fung, 2016b)) but with limita- tions: (a) a rigorous comparison with the state- of-the-art conventional method examined in Yang et al. (2015) is missing; (b) CNN’s performance in the previous research is not quite clear 2 ; and (c) some important techniques that can improve CNN performance (e.g., using varied-sized filters and dropout regularization (Hinton et al.

8ad6927d-2381-4ce5-be2c-4847418114ab.label: text_chunk
8ad6927d-2381-4ce5-be2c-4847418114ab.text:
, 2012)) were not applied. Therefore, the present study is meant to address these limitations.

3 TED Talk Data

TED Talks 3 are recordings from TED confer- ences and other special TED programs. In the present study, we focused on the transcripts of the talks. Most transcripts of the talks contain the markup ‘(Laughter)’, which represents where au- diences laughed aloud during the talks.

64ea2f99-6531-486e-93ba-60bd753692a5.label: text_chunk
64ea2f99-6531-486e-93ba-60bd753692a5.text:
This spe- cial markup was used to determine utterance la- bels.We collected 1 , 192 TED Talk transcripts 4 . An example transcription is given in Figure 1.

 The collected transcripts were split into sentences us- ing the Stanford CoreNLP tool (Manning et al., 2014).

446f1ec7-f9db-4b59-8020-735c97d43f67.label: text_chunk
446f1ec7-f9db-4b59-8020-735c97d43f67.text:
In this study, sentences containing or im- mediately followed by ‘(Laughter)’ were used as ‘Laughter’ sentences, as shown in Figure 1; all other sentences were defined as ‘No-Laughter’ sentences. Following Mihalcea and Strapparava (2005) and Yang et al.

 (2015), we selected the same numbers ( n = 4726 ) of ‘Laughter’ and ‘No- Laughter’ sentences.

3c2dce66-b836-45c0-aecc-3a53f87f090d.label: text_chunk
3c2dce66-b836-45c0-aecc-3a53f87f090d.text:
To minimize possible topic shifts between positive and negative instances, for each positive instance, we picked one negative instance nearby (the context window was 7 sen- tences in this study).

 For example, in Figure 1, a negative instance (corresponding to ‘sent-2’) was selected from the nearby sentences ranging from ‘sent-7’ to ‘sent+7’.

4 Methods

4.1 Conventional Model

Following Yang et al.

2d434e5b-902a-46aa-8191-92acf2add540.label: text_chunk
2d434e5b-902a-46aa-8191-92acf2add540.text: (2015), we applied Random Forest (Breiman, 2001) to perform humor recog- nition by using the following two groups of fea- tures. The first group are latent semantic structural features covering the following 4 categories 5 : In- congruity ( 2 ), Ambiguity ( 6 ), Interpersonal Effect ( 4 ), and Phonetic Pattern ( 4 ).

e9478a7d-471e-4685-be09-58d4d304ea80.label: text_chunk
e9478a7d-471e-4685-be09-58d4d304ea80.text: The second group are semantic distance features, including the hu- mor label classes from 5 sentences in the train- ing set that are closest to this sentence (found by using a k -Nearest Neighbors (kNN) method), and each sentence’s averaged Word2Vec representa- tions ( n = 300 ). More details can be found in Yang et al. (2015).

b7b42f4e-22ee-4adf-b58c-4e4d48add88c.label: text_chunk
b7b42f4e-22ee-4adf-b58c-4e4d48add88c.text: 1 While we were working on this paper, we found a recent Master’s thesis (Acosta, 2016) that also conducted research on detecting laughter on the TED transcriptions. However, that study only explored conventional text classification ap- proaches.2 Though CNN works best when using both lexical and acoustic cues, it did not outperform the Logistical Regression (LR) model when using text inputs exclusively.3 http://www.ted.com

 and he only cares about two things: easy and fun.

sent+7 . . .

482728f1-9534-46ff-88f2-5613fb04965e.text:
Figure 1: An excerpt from TED talk “Tim Urban: Inside the mind of a master procrastinator” ( http: //bit.ly/2l1P3RJ )

4.2 CNN model

Our CNN-based text classification’s setup follows Kim (2014). Figure 2 depicts the model’s details.

bcc0d286-5ae3-433e-ae50-c1dfa3ae30f0.label: text_chunk
bcc0d286-5ae3-433e-ae50-c1dfa3ae30f0.text:
From the left side’s input texts to the right side’s prediction labels, different shapes of tensors flow through the entire network for solving the classifi- cation task in an end-to-end mode.

Firstly, tokenized text strings were converted to a 2 D tensor with shape ( L × d ) , where L rep- resents sentences’ maximum length while d rep- resents the word-embedding dimension.

38081633-7afb-4d14-a841-7f11eb0a50ff.label: text_chunk
38081633-7afb-4d14-a841-7f11eb0a50ff.text:
In this study, we utilized the Word2Vec (Mikolov et al., 2013) embedding vectors ( d = 300 ) that were trained on 100 billion words of Google News.

 Next, the embedding matrix was fed into a 1 D convolution network with multiple filters. To cover varied reception fields, we used filters of sizes of f w − 1 , f w , and f w + 1 .

2304c147-9ac4-4c99-9047-a125b90f35fe.label: text_chunk
2304c147-9ac4-4c99-9047-a125b90f35fe.text:
For each fil- ter size, n f filters were utilized.

 Then, max pool- ing, which stands for finding the largest value from a vector, was applied to each feature map (to- tal 3 × n f feature maps) output by the 1 D con- volution.

af9bf1b3-5dba-4a3c-a728-b71a9453d31e.label: text_chunk
af9bf1b3-5dba-4a3c-a728-b71a9453d31e.text:
Finally, maximum values from all of 3 × n f filters were formed as a flattened vector to go through a fully connected (FC) layer to predict two possible labels (Laughter vs. No-Laughter).

 Note that for 1 D convolution and FC layer’s in- put, we applied ‘dropout’ (Hinton et al., 2012) regularization, which entails randomly setting a proportion of network weights to be zero during model training, to overcome over-fitting.

4887575f-555a-4d1b-a4aa-90c33a47e71e.label: text_chunk
4887575f-555a-4d1b-a4aa-90c33a47e71e.text:
By using cross-entropy as the learning metric, the whole se- quential network (all weights and bias) could be optimized by using any SGD optimization, e.g.

, Adam (Kingma and Ba, 2014), Adadelta (Zeiler, 2012), and so on.

5 Experiments

We used two corpora: the TED Talk corpus (de- noted as TED) and the Pun of the Day corpus 6 (denoted as Pun).

7c1fb966-b168-4763-ba8d-da0acfa8cc6d.label: text_chunk
7c1fb966-b168-4763-ba8d-da0acfa8cc6d.text:
Note that we normalized words in the Pun data to lowercase to avoid a possibly elevated result caused by a special pattern: in the original format, all negative instances started with capital letters. The Pun data allows us to ver- ify that our implementation is consistent with the work reported in Yang et al. (2015).

In our experiment, we firstly divided each cor- pus into two parts.

ceedbc04-a621-4ffe-8d58-e3c5cb6ba14e.label: text_chunk
ceedbc04-a621-4ffe-8d58-e3c5cb6ba14e.text: The smaller part (the Dev set) was used for setting various hyper-parameters used in text classifiers. The larger portion (the CV set) was then formulated as a 10 -fold cross- validation setup for obtaining a stable and com- prehensive model evaluation result. For the PUN data, the Dev contains 482 sentences, while the CV set contains 4344 sentences.

b212f2a2-c642-4eec-8223-f0a693312e91.label: text_chunk
b212f2a2-c642-4eec-8223-f0a693312e91.text: For the TED data, the Dev set contains 1046 utterances, while the CV set contains 8406 utterances. Note that, with a goal of building a speaker-independent humor de- tector, when partitioning our TED data set, we al- ways kept all utterances of a single talk within the same partition.

3cf0af99-743c-4fc0-950a-8f3994a86ffd.label: text_chunk
3cf0af99-743c-4fc0-950a-8f3994a86ffd.text:
To our knowledge, this is the first time that such a strict experimental setup has been used in recognizing humor in conversations, and it makes the humor recognition task on the TED data quite challenging.When building conventional models, we de- veloped our own feature extraction scripts and used the SKLL 7 python package for building Ran- dom Forest models. When implementing CNN,

14847d6f-be64-436c-a775-ba39c6b66d22.text: 9 Regarding hyper-parameter tweaking, we utilized the Tree Parzen Estimation (TPE) method as detailed in Bergstra et al. (2013). After running 200 itera- tions of tweaking, we ended up with the follow- ing selection: f w is 6 (entailing that the vari- ous filter sizes are (5 , 6 , 7) ), n f is 100 , dropout 1 is 0 .

633f1675-d86c-4c2e-a791-304b51c2649e.label: text_chunk
633f1675-d86c-4c2e-a791-304b51c2649e.text: 7 and dropout 2 is 0 . 35 , optimization uses Adam (Kingma and Ba, 2014). When training the CNN model, we randomly selected 10% of the training data as the validation set for using early stopping to avoid over-fitting.On the Pun data, the CNN model shows consis- tent improved performance over the conventional model, as suggested in Yang et al. (2015).

de34c26c-5a86-4b98-b63d-60170ce23d05.label: text_chunk
de34c26c-5a86-4b98-b63d-60170ce23d05.text: In par- ticular, precision has been greatly increased from 0 . 762 to 0 . 864 . On the TED data, we also ob- served that the CNN model helps to increase pre- cision (from 0 . 515 to 0 . 582 ) and accuracy (from 52 . 0% to 58 . 9% ).

399a5939-cb66-471e-9cc0-66e6ce70ae3f.label: text_chunk
399a5939-cb66-471e-9cc0-66e6ce70ae3f.text:
The empirical evaluation results suggest that the CNN-based model has an advan- tage on the humor recognition task.

 In addition, focusing on the system development time, gener-
ating and implementing those features in the con- ventional model would take days or even weeks. However, the CNN model automatically learns its optimal feature representation and can adjust the features automatically across data sets. This makes the CNN model quite versatile for support- ing different tasks and data domains.

d6b1c070-ec75-4176-b02b-23ea9b8ee135.label: text_chunk
d6b1c070-ec75-4176-b02b-23ea9b8ee135.text:
Compared with the humor recognition results on the Pun data, the results on the TED data are still quite low, and more research is needed to fully handle humor in authentic presentations.

6 Discussion

For the purpose of monitoring how well speakers can use humor during their presentations, we have created a corpus from TED talks.

f26473dd-3fbd-4213-a44e-a1840dc8be12.label: text_chunk
f26473dd-3fbd-4213-a44e-a1840dc8be12.text: Compared to the existing (albeit limited) corpora for humor recog- nition research, ours has the following advantages: (a) it was collected from authentic talks, rather than from TV shows performed by professional actors based on scripts; (b) it contains about 100 times more speakers compared to the limited num- ber of actors in existing corpora.

1cf2f81b-4244-4b7b-9060-21a79ff284f3.label: text_chunk
1cf2f81b-4244-4b7b-9060-21a79ff284f3.text: We compared two types of leading text-based humor recognition methods: a conventional classifier (e.g., Random Forest) based on human-engineered features vs. an end-to-end CNN method, which relies on its in- herent representation learning. We found that the CNN method has better performance. More im- portantly, the representation learning of the CNN method makes it very efficient when facing new data sets.

66e2b5f2-bf2f-4e85-a890-1cdbf0b55fb7.label: text_chunk
66e2b5f2-bf2f-4e85-a890-1cdbf0b55fb7.text:
Stemming from the present study, we envision that more research is worth pursuing: (a) for pre- sentations, cues from other modalities such as au- dio or video will be included, similar to Bertero and Fung (2016b); (b) context information from multiple utterances will be modeled by using se- quential modeling methods.8 https://github.com/fchollet/keras
9 The implementation will be released with the paper.

**Summary of Relationships Involving MagiCoder**

1. **Testing and Evaluation**: MagiCoder has been rigorously tested on a dataset of approximately 4500 manually revised reports to assess its performance against human revisions. Its effectiveness is further evaluated using benchmarks and performance metrics such as PT, which measures error minimization and recall precision.

2. **Functionality and Purpose**: Designed to assist pharmacologists and pharmacovigilance stakeholders, MagiCoder streamlines the encoding of Adverse Drug Reaction (ADR) reports, significantly reducing the time required for this task. It processes narrative descriptions of suspected ADRs and generates relevant MedDRA terms, enhancing the efficiency of report revisions.

3. **Technological Integration**: MagiCoder utilizes Natural Language Processing (NLP) algorithms to support pharmacovigilance activities and is implemented as a plug-in for VigiFarmaco, extending its functionality. It maps Italian text to the MedDRA dictionary and recognizes terms within the LLT (Lowest Level Terms) framework.

4. **Collaboration and Development**: The software is developed in collaboration with pharmacovigilance experts and is part of a set of voted solutions, indicating its relevance in the coding community. It also collaborates with human experts to improve the coding of adverse events.

5. **User Interaction**: MagiCoder is designed for direct interaction with users, requiring quick responses to queries. It assists human experts by providing tools to identify redundancy errors, thereby enhancing decision-making in data encoding.

6. **Performance and Limitations**: The performance of MagiCoder is influenced by the complexities of the Italian language, particularly due to synonymical groups that affect coding accuracy. It contrasts with part-of-speech methodologies, as it does not consider sentence structure in its analysis.

7. **Future Enhancements**: Suggestions for improvement include integrating a spell checker feature from VigiFarmaco to enhance user input accuracy and reduce errors. Additionally, MagiCoder aims to test its procedures against various medical classification systems, including ICD-9.

Overall, MagiCoder represents a significant advancement in the automation of pharmacovigilance processes, enhancing the efficiency and accuracy of ADR reporting and coding through advanced technology and collaborative development.
